---
title: "Why Correlations Can't Tell Us Whether Muscle Size Causes Strength"
subtitle: "Part I of III — Associations and Their Discontents"
date: "2026-06-13"
categories: [causal inference, muscle physiology, statistics, simulation]
description: >
  Correlations between changes in muscle size and strength vary wildly across
  studies — anywhere from near zero to above 0.9. But this variability tells
  us less about biology than we might think: the same training programme, run
  twice, can produce almost any correlation depending on who you recruit, how
  you measure change, and which causal forces happen to dominate in your sample.
  This article uses interactive simulations to show the three structural reasons
  associations arise — and why none of them, on their own, can tell us whether
  size actually causes strength.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show R code"
filters:
  - shinylive
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key points

- Correlations between changes in muscle size and strength vary from near zero 
  to above 0.9 across studies — and that variability is itself the problem, 
  not the answer.
- A high correlation can emerge even when size has no causal effect on strength 
  whatsoever, through at least three distinct structural mechanisms.
- Even when the causal effect is real, the correlation you observe depends 
  heavily on choices made before the analysis — who you recruit, how you measure 
  change, and what scale you report it on.
- Establishing that size *causes* strength requires a different framework 
  entirely, which we introduce in Part II.
:::

You have probably heard that correlation is not causation. It is one of those phrases that gets repeated so often it has almost lost its meaning — a disclaimer people attach to findings before moving on to interpret them causally anyway. But if everyone knows this, why do we keep reaching for correlations when we want to understand whether muscle growth drives strength gains?

I think the honest answer is that we are trying to answer a causal question and do not always know how to do it properly. Correlations are easy to compute, easy to report, and — crucially — easy to misread as evidence that one thing is causing another. The literature on muscle size and strength is a good example: observed correlations between changes in the two vary anywhere from near zero to above 0.9 across studies, and that variability itself is rarely treated as the puzzle it should be.

The deeper problem is not which correlation we pick, or how cleverly we estimate it. It is that no correlation, however well-constructed, can answer the question we actually care about: does gaining muscle make you stronger?

That question is causal. Correlations are not. This is not a philosophical distinction — it has real consequences for how we interpret training research and design interventions. In this article, we use interactive simulations to make those consequences concrete. We first show how a strong association between size and strength changes can emerge even when one has nothing to do with the other. We then show how, even in a world where size genuinely does cause strength, the number we report can change dramatically depending on choices that have nothing to do with the underlying biology.

---

## Part I — Three Ways Associations Arise

Every systematic association between muscle size (M) and strength (S) traces 
back to one of three causal structures — or, in small samples, to chance alone. 
We can represent these structures with **directed acyclic graphs (DAGs)**, where 
an arrow from A to B means "A is a cause of B." No arrow means no direct causal 
relationship. What makes DAGs useful is that they let us read off, just from the 
diagram, which variables we would expect to be associated — and why.

### Structure 1 — Direct Causation

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'themeVariables': {'primaryColor': '#0a0a0e', 'primaryBorderColor': '#06b6d4', 'primaryTextColor': '#e2e8f0', 'lineColor': '#06b6d4', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#0f0f15', 'fontFamily': 'JetBrains Mono, monospace', 'secondaryColor': '#0f0f15', 'tertiaryColor': '#0f0f15'}, 'flowchart': {'useMaxWidth': true, 'padding': 20}}}%%
flowchart LR
    T["Training"] --> S["Size (S)"]
    S --> F["Strength (F)"]
    T -->|"via neural adaptation"| F
```

Training causes muscle to grow, and that growth directly causes strength to
increase. A correlation between ΔS and ΔF is telling you something real — but
only that the effect *exists*, not how large it is relative to all other pathways.

### Structure 2 — Common Cause (Confounding)

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'themeVariables': {'primaryColor': '#0a0a0e', 'primaryBorderColor': '#06b6d4', 'primaryTextColor': '#e2e8f0', 'lineColor': '#06b6d4', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#0f0f15', 'fontFamily': 'JetBrains Mono, monospace', 'secondaryColor': '#0f0f15', 'tertiaryColor': '#0f0f15'}, 'flowchart': {'useMaxWidth': true, 'padding': 20}}}%%
flowchart TD
    T["Training"] --> S["Size (S)"]
    T --> F["Strength (F)"]
    S -. "spurious association" .-> F
```

Training causes *both* muscle growth and strength gains independently — through
neural adaptations, tendon stiffness changes, motor learning. Size and strength
will be correlated in any dataset, but removing the muscle growth (magically)
would not reduce strength gains at all. **The correlation is real; the causal
interpretation is wrong.**

### Structure 3 — Collider Bias (Selection)

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'themeVariables': {'primaryColor': '#0a0a0e', 'primaryBorderColor': '#06b6d4', 'primaryTextColor': '#e2e8f0', 'lineColor': '#06b6d4', 'edgeLabelBackground': '#0f0f15', 'fontFamily': 'JetBrains Mono, monospace', 'secondaryColor': '#0f0f15', 'tertiaryColor': '#0f0f15'}, 'flowchart': {'useMaxWidth': true, 'padding': 20}}}%%
flowchart LR
    S["Size (S)"] --> P["Progress Score\n(collider)"]
    F["Strength (F)"] --> P
    S -. "spurious path opens\nwhen conditioning on P" .-> F
```

Suppose a study retains only "good responders" — participants above a threshold
on an overall progress metric that combines size and strength gains. Both
contribute to that selection criterion. Conditioning on it — or restricting
analysis to participants above the threshold — **opens a spurious path** between
Size and Strength even if they have zero direct causal relationship. Within the
selected group, those who grew more muscle appear to have gained more strength,
simply because both had to be elevated to survive selection.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The within-person correlation and confounding

The within-person repeated-measures correlation celebrated by Marques et al.
(2025) is actually *more* susceptible to the confounding story, not less. Within
each person, muscle size and neural/tendon adaptations are both driven by the
same training stimulus, on the same timeline, within the same body. A strong
within-person track of both variables rising together is exactly what you would
predict from the confounding DAG. It does not require direct causation.
:::

---

### 🔬 Simulation 1 — Which World Are We In?

Use the controls below to set the strength of each causal pathway and choose a
world type. Notice that even with the **direct effect of size set to zero**, you
can observe r > 0.4 in the confounding world — and an association appears from
nothing in the collider world the moment you restrict to high-progress participants.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 580

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$style(HTML("
    body { font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif; font-size: 13px; }
    .well { background: #f8f8f8; border: 1px solid #ddd; padding: 12px; }
    h4 { font-size: 13px; font-weight: 700; text-transform: uppercase;
         letter-spacing: 0.08em; color: #2c6e8a; margin-bottom: 10px; }
    .stat-label { font-size: 11px; color: #777; text-transform: uppercase;
                  letter-spacing: 0.1em; }
    .stat-value { font-size: 1.4rem; font-weight: 700; color: #2c6e8a; }
  ")),
  fluidRow(
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("World type"),
        radioButtons("world", NULL,
          choices = c(
            "Direct causation"   = "direct",
            "Confounding"        = "confound",
            "Collider / Selection" = "collider"
          ), selected = "direct"
        ),
        hr(),
        h4("Parameters"),
        sliderInput("trainSize", "Training → Size",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.7, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("sizeStr",   "Size → Strength (direct effect)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("trainStr",  "Training → Strength (neural / direct)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.4, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("noise",     "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=1, value=0.4, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("n",         "N (participants)",
                    min=50, max=500, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample", "Resample", class="btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),
    column(8,
      plotOutput("scatter", height="320px"),
      fluidRow(
        column(4, div(class="stat-label", "Observed r(ΔSize, ΔStrength)"),
                  div(class="stat-value", textOutput("r_obs", inline=TRUE))),
        column(4, div(class="stat-label", "Causal share of variance"),
                  div(class="stat-value", textOutput("r_causal", inline=TRUE))),
        column(4, div(class="stat-label", "World"),
                  div(class="stat-value", textOutput("world_label", inline=TRUE)))
      ),
      hr(),
      p(style="font-size:12px;color:#777;",
        "Red points: selected participants (collider world only). ",
        "Dashed line: OLS regression. Shaded band: 95% CI.")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  set.seed(NULL)

  dat <- reactive({
    input$resample
    n   <- input$n
    b_ts <- input$trainSize
    b_sf <- input$sizeStr
    b_tf <- input$trainStr
    sig  <- input$noise
    training <- rnorm(n)

    if (input$world == "direct") {
      dS <- b_ts * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      dF <- b_sf * dS + b_tf * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      selected <- rep(TRUE, n)
    } else if (input$world == "confound") {
      dS <- b_ts * training + sig * rnorm(n)
      dF <- b_tf * training + sig * rnorm(n)   # no size → strength
      selected <- rep(TRUE, n)
    } else {
      # collider: size and strength from independent causes
      dS <- b_ts * rnorm(n) + sig * rnorm(n)
      dF <- b_tf * rnorm(n) + sig * rnorm(n)
      score    <- dS + dF
      threshold <- quantile(score, 0.40)
      selected <- score >= threshold
    }
    data.frame(dS=dS, dF=dF, selected=selected)
  })

  output$scatter <- renderPlot({
    d <- dat()
    d_plot <- if (input$world == "collider") d[d$selected, ] else d

    r_val <- cor(d_plot$dS, d_plot$dF)

    ggplot(d_plot, aes(x=dS, y=dF)) +
      geom_point(
        aes(colour = if(input$world=="collider") "Selected" else "All"),
        alpha=0.45, size=2
      ) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, colour="#c0392b",
                  fill="#e74c3c", alpha=0.15, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      scale_colour_manual(values=c("All"="#2c6e8a","Selected"="#c0392b"),
                          guide="none") +
      labs(
        x = "Δ Muscle size", y = "Δ Strength",
        title = sprintf("Observed r = %.2f", r_val)
      ) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=13) +
      theme(plot.title = element_text(face="bold", colour="#1a1a1a"))
  })

  output$r_obs <- renderText({
    d <- dat()
    d_plot <- if (input$world == "collider") d[d$selected, ] else d
    sprintf("%.2f", cor(d_plot$dS, d_plot$dF))
  })

  output$r_causal <- renderText({
    if (input$world == "direct") {
      total <- input$sizeStr^2 + input$trainStr^2 + input$noise^2 + 1e-4
      sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * input$sizeStr^2 / total)
    } else if (input$world == "confound") {
      "0% (none)"
    } else {
      "Undefined"
    }
  })

  output$world_label <- renderText({
    switch(input$world,
      direct   = "Direct cause",
      confound = "Confounding",
      collider = "Collider"
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

> **Key insight:** In the confounding world, set *Size → Strength* to **0** and
> watch the correlation remain comfortably above r = 0.3–0.5, driven entirely
> by training as a shared cause. In the collider world, Size and Strength are
> generated *independently* — yet restricting to the top 60% of progress scores
> conjures an association from nothing.

---

## Part II — The Scale Problem: Additive vs. Multiplicative Worlds

Suppose we grant, for the sake of argument, that muscle size truly causes
strength — Structure 1 is the true DAG. We would still face a second, independent
problem: **we do not know the functional form of the biological relationship.**

Consider two hypothetical worlds:

**World A — Additive:** Each additional cm³ of muscle adds the same absolute
number of Newtons, regardless of starting size. The relationship between ΔSize
and ΔStrength is linear on absolute scales.

**World B — Multiplicative:** Each additional cm³ of muscle adds the same
*percentage* of current force production. Somebody who starts stronger gains
more absolute Newtons from the same volume growth than somebody who starts
weaker.

Both are biologically plausible — the truth is probably somewhere between them.
But they have radically different implications for what a correlation study
shows, depending on which scale the researcher chooses.

### Why the scale of measurement changes everything

Researchers routinely express both hypertrophy and strength gains as *percentage
changes* from baseline — partly for comparability. But in a multiplicative world,
percentage changes on both sides correlate strongly with each other even if the
absolute relationship is noisy. In an additive world, using percentage changes
introduces a spurious association driven by baseline values: someone who starts
small gains a large percentage from a small absolute gain, while a large person's
identical absolute gain registers as a small percentage. The correlation you
observe is partly about the biology, and partly about where your participants
happened to start.

::: {.callout-note}
## The Marques et al. r = 0.92 through this lens

The within-person r = 0.92 uses percentage changes as the outcome. In a
multiplicative world with within-person comparison (which is *guaranteed* — each
person is compared to themselves), baseline-size noise that would inflate the
percentage-change correlation between subjects is already perfectly controlled.
The high r is more informative about the **data structure** than about the causal
magnitude.
:::

---

### 🔬 Simulation 2 — Does the Scale Lie?

200 participants with varying baseline muscle size and strength. Toggle between
an additive and multiplicative world, then compare the correlations on absolute
vs. percentage-change scales. Slide *baseline variability* up to see the
discrepancy grow.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 620

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$style(HTML("
    body { font-family: 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif; font-size:13px; }
    .well { background:#f8f8f8; border:1px solid #ddd; padding:12px; }
    h4 { font-size:13px; font-weight:700; text-transform:uppercase;
         letter-spacing:0.08em; color:#2c6e8a; margin-bottom:10px; }
    .stat-label { font-size:11px; color:#777; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.1em; }
    .stat-value { font-size:1.3rem; font-weight:700; }
  ")),
  fluidRow(
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("Data-generating world"),
        radioButtons("dgp", NULL,
          choices=c("Additive"="additive","Multiplicative"="multiplicative"),
          selected="additive"),
        hr(),
        h4("Parameters"),
        sliderInput("beta",    "True causal effect (β)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("baseVar", "Baseline variability",
                    min=0.1, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("noise2",  "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=1, value=0.3, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("n2",      "N",
                    min=50, max=500, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample2","Resample",class="btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),
    column(8,
      plotOutput("two_plots", height="340px"),
      hr(),
      fluidRow(
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","r (absolute)"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2c6e8a",textOutput("r_abs",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","r (percentage)"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2d7d46",textOutput("r_pct",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","|r_abs − r_pct|"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#c0392b",textOutput("r_gap",inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","True β"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#555",textOutput("beta_out",inline=TRUE))
        )
      ),
      p(style="font-size:12px;color:#777;margin-top:10px;",
        "The gap between the two correlations is entirely an artefact of scale choice — ",
        "not a change in the underlying biology.")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  sim2 <- reactive({
    input$resample2
    n   <- input$n2
    b   <- input$beta
    bv  <- input$baseVar
    sig <- input$noise2

    S0  <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))
    F0  <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))

    dS_abs <- pmax(0.01, 0.1 + 0.05 * rnorm(n) + sig * 0.03 * rnorm(n))

    if (input$dgp == "additive") {
      dF_abs <- b * dS_abs + sig * 0.08 * rnorm(n)
    } else {
      pct_s  <- dS_abs / S0
      pct_f  <- b * pct_s + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n)
      dF_abs <- pct_f * F0
    }

    data.frame(
      dS_abs = dS_abs,
      dF_abs = dF_abs,
      pct_dS = dS_abs / S0,
      pct_dF = dF_abs / pmax(0.01, F0)
    )
  })

  output$two_plots <- renderPlot({
    d <- sim2()
    r_abs <- cor(d$dS_abs, d$dF_abs)
    r_pct <- cor(d$pct_dS, d$pct_dF)

    p1 <- ggplot(d, aes(dS_abs, dF_abs)) +
      geom_point(colour="#2c6e8a", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, colour="#c0392b",
                  fill="#e74c3c", alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x="Δ Size (absolute)", y="Δ Strength (absolute)",
           title=sprintf("Absolute  —  r = %.2f", r_abs)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold", colour="#2c6e8a"))

    p2 <- ggplot(d, aes(pct_dS, pct_dF)) +
      geom_point(colour="#2d7d46", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE, colour="#c0392b",
                  fill="#e74c3c", alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x="% Δ Size", y="% Δ Strength",
           title=sprintf("Percentage  —  r = %.2f", r_pct)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold", colour="#2d7d46"))

    p1 + p2
  })

  output$r_abs  <- renderText({ d<-sim2(); sprintf("%.2f", cor(d$dS_abs,d$dF_abs)) })
  output$r_pct  <- renderText({ d<-sim2(); sprintf("%.2f", cor(d$pct_dS,d$pct_dF)) })
  output$r_gap  <- renderText({
    d<-sim2()
    sprintf("%.2f", abs(cor(d$dS_abs,d$dF_abs) - cor(d$pct_dS,d$pct_dF)))
  })
  output$beta_out <- renderText({ sprintf("%.2f", input$beta) })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

> In the **additive world**, push baseline variability to 0.8 and watch the
> percentage-change correlation diverge from the absolute one — the two are
> measuring different things. In the **multiplicative world**, the roles reverse.
> The biology did not change; only the analyst's choice of scale did.

---

## Part III — Putting It Together: The Identification Problem

Even if we know the true causal structure *and* the correct scale, a further
problem remains: **a wide range of true causal worlds can produce the same
observed correlation.** The simulation below lets you adjust all parameters
simultaneously. The grey band marks r ∈ [0.85, 0.95] — roughly what Marques
et al. observed. See how many distinct causal configurations land inside it.

```{shinylive-r}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 600

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$style(HTML("
    body { font-family:'Helvetica Neue',sans-serif; font-size:13px; }
    .well { background:#f8f8f8; border:1px solid #ddd; padding:12px; }
    h4 { font-size:13px; font-weight:700; text-transform:uppercase;
         letter-spacing:.08em; color:#2c6e8a; margin-bottom:10px; }
    .stat-label { font-size:11px; color:#777; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.1em; }
    .stat-value { font-size:1.3rem; font-weight:700; }
    #target_msg { font-weight:700; font-size:1rem; padding:6px 12px; border-radius:3px; }
  ")),
  fluidRow(
    column(4,
      wellPanel(
        h4("Causal structure"),
        sliderInput("s3_direct", "Direct effect: Size → Strength",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.4, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_common", "Common cause: Training → both",
                    min=0, max=1, value=0.6, step=0.05),
        h4("Measurement"),
        sliderInput("s3_scale",  "Scale (0 = absolute, 1 = % change)",
                    min=0, max=1, value=1.0, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_bv",     "Baseline variability",
                    min=0.1, max=1, value=0.5, step=0.05),
        h4("Noise & sample"),
        sliderInput("s3_noise",  "Noise",
                    min=0.05, max=0.8, value=0.25, step=0.05),
        sliderInput("s3_n",      "N",
                    min=50, max=500, value=200, step=50),
        actionButton("resample3","Resample",class="btn btn-primary btn-sm")
      )
    ),
    column(8,
      plotOutput("scatter3", height="320px"),
      hr(),
      fluidRow(
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Observed r"),
          div(class="stat-value", textOutput("r3_obs", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Causal fraction"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#2d7d46", textOutput("r3_causal", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          div(class="stat-label","Confound fraction"),
          div(class="stat-value",style="color:#c0392b", textOutput("r3_confound", inline=TRUE))
        ),
        column(3,
          uiOutput("target_msg")
        )
      ),
      p(style="font-size:12px;color:#777;margin-top:10px;",
        "Grey band = target r ∈ [0.85, 0.95] (approx. Marques et al. 2025). ",
        "How many different causal worlds hit the same target?")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {

  sim3 <- reactive({
    input$resample3
    n    <- input$s3_n
    b_d  <- input$s3_direct
    b_c  <- input$s3_common
    sig  <- input$s3_noise
    sc   <- input$s3_scale
    bv   <- input$s3_bv

    training <- rnorm(n)
    S0 <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))
    F0 <- pmax(0.2, 1 + bv * rnorm(n))

    dS_abs <- pmax(0.01, b_c * training * 0.15 + 0.08 + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n))
    dF_direct  <- b_d * dS_abs
    dF_common  <- b_c * training * 0.15
    dF_abs <- dF_direct + dF_common + sig * 0.05 * rnorm(n)

    # blend between absolute and % scale
    x_mix <- (1 - sc) * dS_abs + sc * (dS_abs / S0)
    y_mix <- (1 - sc) * dF_abs + sc * (dF_abs / pmax(0.01, F0))

    list(
      x = x_mix, y = y_mix,
      causal_frac  = b_d^2 / (b_d^2 + b_c^2 + sig^2 + 1e-4),
      confound_frac = b_c^2 / (b_d^2 + b_c^2 + sig^2 + 1e-4)
    )
  })

  output$scatter3 <- renderPlot({
    s   <- sim3()
    r   <- cor(s$x, s$y)
    d   <- data.frame(x=s$x, y=s$y)

    # y-range for target band
    yr  <- range(s$y)
    band_lo <- yr[1] + 0.85 * diff(yr)
    band_hi <- yr[1] + 0.95 * diff(yr)

    ggplot(d, aes(x, y)) +
      annotate("rect",
               xmin=-Inf, xmax=Inf,
               ymin=band_lo, ymax=band_hi,
               fill="grey80", alpha=0.4) +
      annotate("text", x=min(d$x)+0.01*diff(range(d$x)),
               y=mean(c(band_lo,band_hi)),
               label="target r ∈ [0.85, 0.95]",
               hjust=0, size=3, colour="grey50") +
      geom_point(colour="#2c6e8a", alpha=0.35, size=1.8) +
      geom_smooth(method="lm", se=TRUE,
                  colour="#c0392b", fill="#e74c3c",
                  alpha=0.12, linetype="dashed", linewidth=0.8) +
      labs(x = ifelse(input$s3_scale < 0.5, "Δ Size (absolute mix)", "% Δ Size (pct mix)"),
           y = ifelse(input$s3_scale < 0.5, "Δ Strength (absolute mix)", "% Δ Strength (pct mix)"),
           title = sprintf("Observed r = %.2f", r)) +
      theme_minimal(base_size=13) +
      theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold",colour="#1a1a1a"))
  })

  output$r3_obs     <- renderText({ sprintf("%.2f", cor(sim3()$x, sim3()$y)) })
  output$r3_causal  <- renderText({ sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * sim3()$causal_frac) })
  output$r3_confound <- renderText({ sprintf("%.0f%%", 100 * sim3()$confound_frac) })

  output$target_msg <- renderUI({
    r <- cor(sim3()$x, sim3()$y)
    in_target <- r >= 0.85 & r <= 0.95
    div(id="target_msg",
        style=paste0("background:", if(in_target) "#d5f5e3" else "#fdecea",
                     ";color:", if(in_target) "#1e8449" else "#922b21", ";"),
        if(in_target) "✓ In target zone" else "✗ Outside target"
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

> You should find it easy to achieve r > 0.85 even with the direct effect at
> zero, by increasing the common-cause coefficient and switching to percentage
> scaling. Conversely, a substantial direct effect can yield r ≈ 0.3 if baseline
> variability is high and you measure absolute changes in a multiplicative world.
> **The same number can arise from very different data-generating processes.**

---

## What Would Actually Help?

To answer whether muscle size *causally* affects strength, we need one of the
following:

**1. Experimental variation in size independent of training load.** If you could
randomly assign participants to gain different amounts of muscle (via BFR, very
different rep ranges equated for effort, or pharmacological aids) while holding
training volume and motor learning constant, you could estimate the causal
effect. Some designs come close, but unmeasured confounders remain.

**2. A formal causal mediation analysis** — which attempts to decompose the
total effect of training into the portion that runs *through* muscle growth and
the portion that bypasses it (neural adaptations, etc.).

Crucially, however, even causal mediation analysis makes strong assumptions that
are unlikely to hold perfectly. **Part II of this series** will dig into the
mediation framework, and **Part III** will show exactly where those assumptions
break — and what happens to our estimates when they do.

---

## Summary

| Problem | Mechanism | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Confounding | Training drives both size and strength | Randomise size *independently* of training |
| Collider bias | Selecting on a common effect of both | Avoid conditioning on downstream variables |
| Scale artefact | Absolute vs. % change in add./mult. world | Pre-register scale; sensitivity across both |
| Identification | Many causal worlds → same r | Need causal assumptions, not just data |

Correlations between hypertrophy and strength gains arise from at least three
structural mechanisms, any of which can produce arbitrarily high observed
correlations. No correlation statistic — Pearson, repeated-measures, or
otherwise — can distinguish between them without additional assumptions about
the causal graph.

Beyond causal structure, the scale of measurement interacts with the
data-generating process to produce correlations that reflect the researcher's
analytic choices as much as the underlying biology.

The conclusion is not that muscle size does not cause strength — it clearly
contributes. The conclusion is that the correlation coefficient is a blunt,
causally agnostic tool that cannot tell us how much it contributes, under what
conditions, or for whom. For that, we need a different framework.

*That is where we are going next.*

---

*Simulations are stochastic; resample to build intuition across runs. All data
are synthetic.*
